generated_comment: Yaar, ultimately it's about what works for your family na? For us, it's just become natural for me to handle ghar ka sab and kids. Raj takes care of all the outside work and finances. Honestly, it's more practical this way. Plus, he helps pick up the kids from school sometimes, which is a huge relief!
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Anita, a working woman, defends her household's gendered division of labor as 'natural' and 'practical'. She assumes full responsibility for domestic work and childcare while her husband manages external tasks and finances, framing this as an efficient choice rather than a traditional imposition. She also celebrates her husband's minimal contribution (picking up kids) as a significant help, indicating a low bar for male participation in domestic duties.
original_frame: Motherhood as Basis of Respect, Women Defined by Male Relations
persona_text: You are Anita. Age 39. Married with two children. A working woman in Delhi. You are at your office and a younger female colleague named Shreya who is 31 and single has just been announced as the new team head. You are on a WhatsApp call with your closest friend after office hours. You are talking about Shreya's promotion and what you think of her as a person and a leader and in the course of the conversation you reveal that you do not fully respect Shreya as a woman because she has no husband and no children and you are not sure what kind of decisions she can make for a team when she has no real responsibilities at home.
